Q15 Composing A:
I write full compositions entirely within a digital audio workstation (DAW). I typically use a template that sets up drum samples, guitar/bass tones already ready to go with basic mix moves already applied. I usually start with guitar, come up with a riff, write drums for it, then slowly build out the song from there. At some point I will write vocals, usually by singing nonsense words until I get a melody and lyrics that I like. I record the vocals directly into the DAW while writing the demo, with harmonies and everything as well. After a while I will have a fully composed song in the DAW, with intricate drum parts, synths, samples, etc. For my best songs, I then would hire a drummer to record real drums, and then rerecord the guitar/bass/vocals myself for the final version.

As for software, I use REAPER as my DAW. I use mostly Neural DSP for guitar/bass tones, GetGoodDrums for drum samples, and Melodyne for vocal tuning. I probably can't list out every plugin I use, but I do like to use a lot of the Slate and Fabfilter plugins.

Q40 Use Techniques_10_TEXT: False cord, fry, and arytenoid cartilage. These are the only 3 tissues in the human vocal tract that we can use to safely perform distorted vocal techniques. Everything else on the list are just sub-variants or styles of FC, Fry, and AC based distortion.
Q41 Other Techniques: Basically there are 3 ways to safely produce vocal distortion. Fry screams are produced by the true cords. False cord screams are produced by vibrating the false cords, and a lesser known style where you can vibrate the arytenoid cartilage to produce distortion(similar to how Jazz singers perform the Jazz Growl). FC and Fry screams can be performed simultaneously as the true cords and false cords are positioned so closely together in the vocal tract.

Q59:
I think that the best terminology to convey extreme vocals is as follows.

1.) start with the vocal tissue used to produce the distortion. Fry = true cords, FC = false cords. AC = arytenoid cartilage. Some people talk about other tissues like the epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds as sources of distortion but these have rarely been demonstrated in endoscopy studies and are not viable in long term practice. 

2.) Fry and false cord allow you to perform almost the exact same things. You can perform lows, mids, and highs with each technique. You can perform pitched screams with distinguishable notes present with both techniques. arytenoid cartilage can only be used to produce low pitched screams. 

3.)Because each tissue results in different distorted sounds, it is best to classify them based on the actual tissue used to produce the distortion, and then any modifiers to further describe the style. For instance, Fry, FC and AC would be considered distortion techniques. Goblin, zombie, tunnel throat, guttural, dinosaur (and so on) would be considered vocal styles that can be achieved using the techniques.

Q15 Composing A: I use FL studio, which made sense when I was into electronic music, but a lot less sense after getting into metal LOL. I still use it due to the lifetime free updates. I generally compose parts in MIDI with placeholder instruments first, keeping in mind my ability to later play those parts on guitar and bass. I'll also compose the vocal parts using a temporary midi instrument and write the lyrics in a document, which makes it convenient to import into singing synth software, or use as reference for recording myself. Generally I don't record audio tracks until the composition and arrangement is already done. I'll practice playing until I can perform it reasonably well enough to record in minimal takes, then go through and punch in retakes of bars with mistakes. Occasionally I need more practice for a part, so I'll export the midi and reformat it as TAB for my reference.

Q6: I've been surrounded by it my whole life. Both of my parents were very amateur musicians.
Q7:
My parents were big fans of the classic metal of the late 70's and 80's, all the way until the early 2000's. My eldest brother was very into the late 90's early 2000's Death/metalcore, as he was the approperiate age to see and actively participate in the Myspace wave of those genres.

What really inspired me was my association with my friends, I wasn't super into extreme music like my brother was until I lied to some soon-to-be friends about my experience and knowledge as a musician. They they exposed me fully into that world, and that is what got me lost into it.
Q9 Metal Subgenres A:
The vocals are honestly the most important part to me. Nearly all of these genres have people that sound very unique and push what the human voice is capable of.

The next most important thing to me, is how much groove the instrumental has, and how it all builds and flows together.
Q10A Non-metal subge:
Hip-hop/rap is because my mom and brothers also enjoy it, and I really enjoy the wordplay and how much structured and layered meaning the words can have. 

Classical is because my training as a musician. I was in concert band for 8 years, and was exposed to a lot of classical music.
Q13 Instruments A: Guitar and bass for roughly 10 years, drums for 7 years off and on, and vocals for 9 years.
Q15 Composing A:
I tend to pick a scale of some sort and build a chord structure using that scale to some arbitrarily chosen rhythm. I tend to write rhythm guitar and drums simultaneously, typically hand programming the guitar notes on a midi-piano and then using that to teach it to myself on guitar. Due to lack of equipment I write drums the same way. 

the DAW i use is Reaper, and I use various samples/midi controllers for instruments that I do not physically possess. I tend to use amp sims for any guitar related work.
